tool.name: SOPA Annotate

param.name: marker_cell_dict
param.label: Marker to cell-type dictionary
param.help: JSON dictionary mapping channel/marker names to cell types. Format: {"MARKER1": "Cell_Type1", "MARKER2": "Cell_Type2"}. Example: {"CD3": "T cell", "CD20": "B cell", "CK": "Tumor cell"}

param.name: reference_adata
param.label: Reference single-cell dataset
param.help: AnnData object (h5ad file) containing annotated single-cell reference data with cell-type labels.

param.name: marker_list
param.label: Marker gene list (comma-separated, optional)
param.help: Optional: List of marker genes to use for mapping. Leave empty to use all genes.

param.name: mode
param.label: Tangram mode
param.option: Cells - Map to individual reference cells

tool.help:
**What it does**
SOPA Annotate assigns cell-type labels to segmented and aggregated cells. Two annotation strategies are supported:
1. **Fluorescence/Channel Z-score**: Simple marker-based annotation using fluorescence intensity 2. **Tangram**: Advanced annotation using reference single-cell RNA-seq data
**Requirements**
- Input must be an aggregated SpatialData object (output from SOPA Aggregate) - Must have aggregated either channels (for fluorescence method) or genes (for Tangram)
**Fluorescence/Channel Z-score Method**
This simple but effective method assigns cell types based on the brightest marker channel. Each cell is labeled with the cell type whose corresponding marker has the highest Z-score (standardized intensity).
**Input format**: JSON dictionary mapping markers to cell types
Example::
{"CD3": "T cell", "CD20": "B cell", "CD68": "Macrophage", "CK": "Tumor cell"}
**How it works**: 1. For each cell, calculate Z-scores for all specified markers 2. Identify which marker has the highest Z-score 3. Assign the corresponding cell type to that cell
**Advantages**: - Simple and interpretable - Fast computation - Works well for multiplexed imaging with specific markers - No reference data required
**Limitations**: - Only considers one marker per cell type - Cannot handle complex cell states or subtypes - May miss cells expressing multiple markers
**Best for**: MACSima, PhenoCycler, CosMx (with fluorescence), or any dataset with clear marker-cell type associations
**Tangram Method**
Tangram is a sophisticated method that maps spatial data to reference single-cell RNA-seq data using machine learning. It learns the spatial distribution of cell types based on gene expression similarity.
**Input**: Reference AnnData object with cell-type annotations in `.obs`
**Modes**: - **Clusters** (default): Map to reference cell-type clusters (faster, more robust) - **Cells**: Map to individual reference cells (more detailed, slower) - **Constrained**: Incorporate spatial constraints for more biologically plausible mapping
**Advantages**: - Can leverage large reference atlases - Handles complex cell types and states - Provides probabilistic cell-type assignments - Can identify novel cell types not in reference
**Limitations**: - Requires high-quality reference data - Computationally intensive - May be sensitive to batch effects - Requires overlapping genes between spatial and reference data
**Best for**: Xenium, MERSCOPE, CosMx (transcript-based), Visium HD, or any spatial transcriptomics with matching reference data
**Reference Data Requirements**: - AnnData format (h5ad file) - Must contain cell-type annotations in `.obs` - Should have overlapping genes with spatial data - Ideally from same tissue type
**Output**
The tool adds cell-type annotations to the AnnData table within the SpatialData object: - New column in `.obs` containing predicted cell types - For Tangram: Probability scores for each cell type
**Combining Methods**
For best results, you can run both methods and compare: 1. Use fluorescence annotation for quick exploration 2. Use Tangram for detailed cell typing 3. Compare results to validate findings
**More Complex Annotation**
For more sophisticated annotation strategies: - Export AnnData to h5ad format - Use specialized tools like Leiden clustering + manual annotation - Use tools like CellTypist or Azimuth - Combine multiple annotation approaches
**More Information**
See the `SOPA annotation documentation <https://prism-oncology.github.io/sopa/tutorials/cli_usage/#annotation>`_.
